title: 3 U.S. Service Members Killed, 5 Seriously Wounded in Operation Epic Fury
description: This post has been updated with additional information from U.S. CENTCOM and maritime Joint Maritime Information Center. text:
This post has been updated with additional information from U.S. CENTCOM and maritime Joint Maritime Information Center.
Three U.S. service members were killed and five seriously wounded as part of the U.S. strikes in Iran, U.S. Central Command announced Sunday morning.
The post on social media said those killed and injured were part of Operation Epic Fury, but did not offer additional details.
“​​Several others sustained minor shrapnel injuries and concussions — and are in the process of being returned to duty. Major combat operations continue and our response effort is ongoing,” reads the statement. “The situation is fluid, so out of respect for the families, we will withhold additional information, including the identities of our fallen warriors, until 24 hours after next of kin have been notified.”
President Donald Trump addressed the deaths in a Sunday afternoon video message on social media.
“Earlier today, CENTCOM shared the news that three US military service members have been killed in action as one nation, we grieve for the true American patriots who have made the ultimate sacrifice for our nation, even as we continue the righteous mission for which they gave their lives, we pray for the full recovery of the wounded and send our immense love and eternal gratitude to the families of the fallen,” Trump said. “Sadly, there will likely be more before it ends.”
On Saturday, U.S. and Israeli Defense Forces launched a series of massive strikes on military and leadership targets in Iran, launching from bases in the region as well as USS Abraham Lincoln (CVN-72), which is operating in the North Arabian Sea. The strikes resulted in the death of senior Iranian leaders, including Iran’s sectarian leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
In response, Iranian forces fired multiple waves of drone and missile attacks toward Israel, Jordon, UAE, Iraq, Saudi Arabia, Qatar and Bahrain. Those included strikes on U.S. 5th Fleet’s headquarters in Manama, Bahrain, and the U.S. Al Udeid Air Base in Qatar. The initial targets included U.S. military assets in those countries but expanded to include civilian buildings and infrastructure.
Announcements of the casualties come as air and missile attacks have continued across the region throughout the second day of Epic Fury.
The Israeli aerial campaign has struck more air defense and missile sites throughout Western Iran in a move to access key targets in the interior of the country. Those include command and control centers in Tehran. Israeli aircraft have also been tasked to hunt down Iranian mobile missile launchers before their operators could fire their weapons. The Israel Defense Force claims that its operations at the onset of Epic Fury — and known by Israel as Roaring Lion — was the country’s largest aerial operation to date.
Ongoing U.S. strikes include the sinking of an Iranian Navy Jamaran-class corvette at its berthing in Chabahar Port, U.S. Central Command said Sunday morning.
Iran expanded its strikes to Oman, which attempted to mediate between Washington and Tehran before hostilities began. One-way attack drones struck Oman’s primary port, injuring a dockworker.
At least 282 Iranian missiles and 833 drones have been intercepted by Kuwaiti, Qatari, Bahraini and Emirati air defense forces, according to each Gulf State’s defense ministry.
U.S. Central Command Releases Additional Attack Details
The U.S. strikes on Iran began at 1:15 a.m. and hit over 1,000 targets, according to a fact sheet released by CENTCOM on Sunday.
The strikes involved B-2 Spirit stealth bombers, Army PAC3 interceptor missile systems, LUCAS drones, THAAD anti-ballistic missile systems, F-35 Lighting II stealth fighters, Air Force A-10 Thunderolt IIs, Navy EA-18G Growler electronic attack aircraft, airborne early warning and control aircraft, airborne communication relay, P-8A Posiedon maritime patrol aircraft, RC-135 reconnaissance aircraft, MQ-9 Reapers, M-142 High Mobility Artillery Rocket Systems (HIMARS), counter-drone systems, refueling tanker aircraft, C-17 Globemaster cargo aircraft, C-130 cargo aircraft and F/A-18 E/F Super Hornets, F-16 Falcona and F-22 Raptor fighters.
“And special capabilities we can’t list here!” reads the fact sheet.
Stalled Strait
While Iran has claimed it closed the Strait of Hormuz, it remains open, maritime expert Sal Mercogliano posted on social media site X. However, traffic has stalled in the strait as companies figure out war insurance needed for the ships to pass through the waters, Mercogliano said. The Joint Maritime Information Center puts the risk level of the strait as critical, meaning an attack is “almost certain” to happen, according to the advisory.
According to the JMIC, there have been three confirmed maritime attacks – MT Skylight attacked near Khasab, Oman, MT MKD VYOM near Muscat, Oman and MT Sea La Donna. Skylight’s crew evacuated and reported four injuries.
A crew member in MKD VYOM’s engine room also died, Reuters reported. MT Hercules Star also reported a fire after being struck by an unknown projectile, according to the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations.
USNI News staff writer Heather Mongilio contributed to this post.
